problem_solved: How can an overly broad request be narrowed into a task that can actually be executed reliably?
core_idea: Constrain the task boundary before deciding the execution path.
method_structure: Identify the true scope of the request.
design_choices: The method prioritizes scope stability over early coverage.
hidden_assumptions: The operator can judge which goals really belong inside the current task boundary.
fit_scenarios: Useful when the task keeps expanding or mixing multiple objectives.
non_fit_scenarios: Less useful when the request is already precise and constrained.
comparison_with_alternatives: Compared with broad exploratory prompting, this method sacrifices spread to gain execution stability.
common_misuses: Over-narrowing too early and discarding constraints that actually matter.
failure_modes: If the scope is narrowed too hard, the output can become stable but miss the real goal.
decision_criteria: If the boundary is still unclear, do not expand implementation yet.
representative_examples: Turning 'optimize my whole knowledge system' into 'first design the progression protocol for one method skill'.
validation_question: Has the current request really been narrowed to one clear output target?

upstream_concepts: [[Problem Framing]] | Prompt narrowing depends on a clear framing of the task boundary.
upstream_mechanisms: [[Scope Drift]] | The method becomes easier to justify once scope drift is understood.
adjacent_methods: [[Prompt Iteration]] | Both improve prompts, but prompt narrowing acts earlier on scope.

prerequisites: [[Single Output Target]] | Without a single output target, narrowing cannot stabilize execution.
enables: [[Task Execution]] | Once the scope is narrowed, execution paths become easier to stabilize.
contrasts: [[Broad Prompt Exploration]] | One expands possibility space; the other reduces it.
corrections: [[More Scope Means Better Results]] | This method corrects the intuition that broader scope always helps.

direct_routes: When the request still mixes multiple goals, route to [[Single Output Target]] before continuing this method.
If the boundary is unclear because the problem itself is vague, return to [[Problem Framing]].
secondary_routes: If [[Problem Framing]] clarifies the task but execution still drifts, continue to [[Scope Drift]] and then return here.
gap_signals: If the graph contains scoping methods but no card explains how to choose between them, add a new Method card for scoping-method selection.
stop_rules: Stop applying this method once the main problem is no longer scope but execution or causal diagnosis.

current_status_notes: The card is already a reliable local method node and now has an explicit dispatch skeleton.
next_goal: Add stronger failure fallback rules for when narrowing does not stabilize execution.
growing_checklist: Explain where the method fits and where it does not.
stable_checklist: Provide at least three strong node-level relationships.
Explain the method's local graph position.
expert_ready_checklist: Add stronger cross-card fallback and method-arbitration rules.
Add at least one explicit gap signal and one stop rule.

main_reasons: The method already has strong local graph value.
It now has direct routes, but not enough dispatch depth for expert-ready.
missing_evidence: Fallback behavior after method failure is still too weak.
It lacks stronger method-selection arbitration across adjacent strategies.
next_rules: When the method fails because the task is misframed, return to [[Problem Framing]].
When the method fails because the target is still unstable, return to [[Single Output Target]].

current_upgrade_tasks: Add one case where prompt narrowing reduced scope but still failed because the real cause was elsewhere.
upgrade_history: 2026-03-27: migrated routing to the four-part dispatch structure
